ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP represents paneer as a dairy product; Paneer Chukka is a prepared dish using paneer as the primary ingredient. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of Paneer Chukka is approximately 200 g, representing a single-portion side dish or main course accompaniment served with bread or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Paneer (Cottage Cheese) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for prepared paneer dish estimation
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Paneer Chukka is a prepared dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) for paneer (cottage cheese) as the primary ingredient, combined with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for onion, oil, and spice contributions. The dish is predominantly paneer (~55–60% by weight) with onion, oil, and spices. Values represent a composite estimate for the cooked dish per 100 g. Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate are weighted composites. Sodium reflects added salt and spice blend typical of restaurant preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; University of Sydney GI Database entry for paneer / Indian cottage cheese
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Paneer Chukka as a prepared dish. GI of 27 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for paneer (Indian cottage cheese), which is the dominant ingredient (~55–60% by weight). Paneer has a low GI due to its high protein and fat content with minimal carbohydrate. The stir-fried preparation with onion and spices does not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (27 × 6.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 3.5 g, confirming very low GL. gi_category set to 'low' per threshold rule (GI ≤ 55).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from added salt and spice blends in restaurant preparation
hypertension.guidance: A 200 g restaurant serving of Paneer Chukka contributes approximately 640 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake below 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (GI 27) and low glycemic load per serving (GL ~3.5) due to high protein and fat content of paneer
type2_diabetes.guidance: Paneer Chukka has a very low GI of 27 and a glycemic load of approximately 3.5 per serving, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions without high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as naan or rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content from paneer and cooking oil; cholesterol contribution from full-fat dairy
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each 200 g serving provides approximately 14.8 g saturated fat and 76 mg cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, request preparation with minimal oil, and balance with high-fibre vegetables to mitigate saturated fat impact on LDL cholesterol.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Paneer Chukka is a popular North Indian dry-style dish made from paneer (Indian cottage cheese) that is cubed and stir-fried or pan-tossed with onions, green chillies, lime juice, and a blend of aromatic spices including cumin, turmeric, coriander powder, garam masala, ginger, and garlic. The term 'chukka' (also spelled 'sukka' or 'sukha') refers to a dry preparation style in South and North Indian cooking, distinguishing it from gravy-based paneer dishes. The dish has a firm, slightly charred exterior on the paneer cubes with a fragrant, spiced coating. The texture is chewy and satisfying, with the paneer retaining its mild milky flavour beneath the spice crust. It is commonly served as a starter, side dish, or accompaniment to Indian flatbreads such as roti, naan, or paratha. In Singapore, it is offered at Indian restaurants including tandoor-style establishments. Nutritionally, Paneer Chukka is protein-rich (~11 g per 100 g) and high in calcium (~190 mg per 100 g) due to the paneer base. It has a very low glycemic index (GI ~27) making it suitable for blood glucose management. The dish is vegetarian and gluten-free. Sodium content is moderate to high in restaurant versions (~320 mg per 100 g), which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension. The saturated fat content (~7.4 g per 100 g) warrants moderation for those with cardiovascular disease risk.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Paneer Chukka?
Is Paneer Chukka good for diabetics?
What is the difference between Paneer Chukka and Paneer Butter Masala?
What does chukka mean in Indian cooking?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for paneer as the primary ingredient combined with USDA SR Legacy data for the cooked dish components. No exact ICMR-NIN entry for Paneer Chukka as a prepared dish exists; values are composite estimates. GI is analogously estimated from University of Sydney GI Database paneer entry. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted/estimated nature of the nutrient profile.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for paneer or this dish category. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for paneer and cooking oil components; these are not directly reported in ICMR-NIN for this dish. The dish description from the input source (field survey data, Mr Tandoor - Boat Quay) mentions mint chutney as an accompaniment; nutrient values here represent the Paneer Chukka dish only, excluding the chutney. Restaurant sodium levels may vary significantly. GI of 27 is an analogous estimate based on paneer as the dominant ingredient per University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Paneer Chukka as a prepared dish was found in the approved sources.
